1: לָמָּה תִּתְרָאוּ (lāmmāh titrāʾû) — "why do you just keep looking at each other?" The Hitpael of rāʾāh (to see oneself, to look at one another — the reflexive-reciprocal form; Hitpael implies mutual, repeated looking: they are staring at each other without acting). The idiom expresses paralytic indecision in a crisis — looking around for someone else to take initiative. Jacob's blunt rhetorical question breaks the paralysis: the patriarch names the situation and commands action.
2: רְדוּ-שָׁמָּה וְשִׁבְרוּ-לָנוּ מִשָּׁם (rĕḏû-šāmmāh wĕšiḇrû-lānû miššām) — "go down there and buy for us from there." Rāḏaḏ (to go down — cf. the later "going down" to Egypt by Jacob [Genesis 46:3-4]; the geographical descent from Canaan to Egypt is built into the Hebrew verb). Šāḇar (to buy grain — the grain-commerce verb of Genesis 41:56-57; Jacob uses the technical term of Joseph's operation). וְנִחְיֶה וְלֹא נָמוּת (that we may live and not die) — the stark life-or-death alternative; ḥāyāh (to live) versus mût (to die); the existential famine-demand reduced to its essence.
3: עֲשֶׂרֶת אֲחֵי יוֹסֵף (ʿăśeret ʾăḥê yôsēp, the ten brothers of Joseph) — the narrator identifies them by their relationship to Joseph, not to Jacob: "Joseph's brothers," not "Jacob's sons." The perspective is already moving toward Joseph's vantage point. The number ten is prominent: Benjamin is withheld; the eleven of the dream-vision will only be complete when Benjamin arrives in Egypt.
4: פֶּן-יִקְרָאֶנּוּ אָסוֹן (pen-yiqrāʾennû ʾāsôn) — "lest harm come to him." ʾĀsôn (harm, fatal disaster — a rare word appearing only 5 times in the OT: here, Genesis 42:38; Genesis 44:29 [twice]; Exodus 21:22-23; in each context it refers to a serious physical harm, possibly death; cf. Exodus 21:22-23 where ʾāsôn distinguishes minor injury from fatal harm in the pregnant-woman scenario). The word Jacob uses for his fear about Benjamin is the same word he will use when refusing to send Benjamin (Genesis 42:38; Genesis 44:29) — the vocabulary of his protective anxiety forms a refrain.
5: כִּי-הָיָה הָרָעָב בְּאֶרֶץ כְּנָעַן — "for the famine was in the land of Canaan also." The gam (also) of Genesis 42:5 (implied in "also") connects Canaan to the universal scope of the famine that Genesis 42:57 described: "all the earth" came to Egypt; now we see that includes Canaan. The sons of Israel are among the international crowd seeking grain — not distinguished by privilege but by the same hunger that drives all nations to Joseph.
6: וַיִּשְׁתַּחֲווּ-לוֹ אַפַּיִם אָרְצָה (wayyištaḥăwû-lô ʾappayim ʾarṣāh) — "they bowed down to him with their faces to the ground." The fulfillment of the first dream: in Genesis 37:7, the brothers' sheaves "bowed down to" Joseph's sheaf (wattištaḥăweynāh... lʾălummāṯî). Now the men themselves bow (wayyištaḥăwû-lô) — the same Hishtaphel of šāḥāh. ʾAppayim ʾarṣāh (nostrils to the ground — the prostration posture; literally "with their nostrils to the ground"); the full prostration, not a nodding bow. Twenty-two years after the dream, its first half is fulfilled without anyone recognizing it.
7: וַיַּכֵּר יוֹסֵף אֶת-אֶחָיו וְהֵם לֹא הִכִּרֻהוּ (wayyakkēr yôsēp ʾet-ʾeḥāyw wĕhēm lōʾ hikkirûhû) — "Joseph recognized his brothers but they did not recognize him." The same recognition-verb (nākar) that Jacob used when "recognizing" the bloody robe (Genesis 37:33: wayyakkirāh) and that the brothers used when sending the robe ("please recognize": hakkēr-nāʾ, Genesis 37:32). Recognition runs through the Joseph narrative: the robe recognized, the brothers recognized but unrecognized. וַיִּתְנַכֵּר אֲלֵיהֶם (wayyitnakkēr ʾălêhem) — "he feigned to be a stranger to them" — Hitpael of nākar: to make oneself unrecognizable, to play the stranger. Joseph actively performs non-recognition; the same root is used both for genuine recognition and for performed concealment.
8: וְיוֹסֵף הִכִּיר אֶת-אֶחָיו וְהֵם לֹא הִכִּרֻהוּ — "Joseph recognized his brothers but they did not recognize him." The repetition of the non-recognition formula from Genesis 42:7 is not redundant but emphatic: the narrator wants the reader to sit with this fact before the narrative continues. The asymmetry of recognition — Joseph knows everything; the brothers know nothing — will drive every subsequent scene until Joseph's disclosure in Genesis 45.
9: וַיִּזְכֹּר יוֹסֵף אֵת הַחֲלֹמוֹת אֲשֶׁר חָלַם לָהֶם (wayyizkōr yôsēp ʾet haḥălōmôt ʾăšer ḥālam lāhem) — "Joseph remembered the dreams he had dreamed about them." Zākar (to remember — the covenantal memory-verb; God "remembers" his covenant; the cupbearer "forgot" Joseph [šāḵaḥ, Genesis 40:23]; now Joseph "remembers" the dreams). The dreams are what he dreams "about them" (lāhem) — the brothers are the content of the dreams; seeing them prostrating triggers the dream-memory. מְרַגְּלִים אַתֶּם (mĕragglîm ʾattem, you are spies) — rāgal (to go about as a spy, to slander; Piel participle: professional spies/scouts); the same root used for the twelve scouts of Num 13 whose negative "report" (dibbāh) of the land echoes Joseph's "bad report" of his brothers in Genesis 37:2.
10: לֹא אֲדֹנִי (lōʾ ʾăḏōnî, no, my lord) — the brothers address Joseph with the submission formula ʾăḏōnî (my lord — the title of social superiority); the same title Jacob used in his message to Esau (Genesis 32:4-5: "my lord Esau... your servant Jacob"). The brothers use the lord-servant language instinctively in the presence of authority; they are speaking to their own brother as "my lord" exactly as the dream predicted their submission. Liḵĕnôt-ʾōḵel (to buy food) — food, not intelligence; the economic desperation motivating the trip is real.
11: כֻּלָּנוּ בְּנֵי אִישׁ אֶחָד נַחְנוּ (kullānû bĕnê ʾîš ʾeḥāḏ nāḥnû) — "we are all sons of one man." The brothers appeal to their family unity as proof of good faith: a united family has no motive for espionage; spies are typically strangers without family connections. Kĕnîm ʾănaḥnû (we are honest men) — kēn (honest, upright, true — the word means "right, correct, honest"; cf. Proverbs 11:19; the same root as the covenant-loyalty word kēn in many contexts). Their claim of honesty (kĕnîm) will be tested by a process that they cannot escape.
12: Joseph's insistence: "No! You have come to see where our land is undefended." ʿErwat hāʾāreṣ (the nakedness/exposed state of the land) — ʿerwāh (nakedness, exposed state — the same word used for sexual exposure in Lev 18-20; here used for military vulnerability: the "nakedness" of a land is its undefended approaches and weak points that a spy would report). Joseph's accusation, though false, has the effect of forcing the brothers into a position where they must prove their identity and family integrity.
13: שְׁנֵים עָשָׂר עֲבָדֶיךָ אַחִים אֲנַחְנוּ (šĕnêm ʿāśār ʿăḇāḏeḵā ʾaḥîm ʾănaḥnû) — "your servants, twelve brothers, we are." The brothers identify themselves as twelve — including the absent Benjamin and the missing-presumed-dead Joseph. Wĕhāʾeḥāḏ ʾênennû (and one is no more) — "is no more" (ʾênennû = he is not; the formula of absence/non-existence; cf. Rachel "is no more" in Genesis 37:30: "the boy is not there"). The brothers use the same idiom that Reuben used when he found the empty cistern — an irony that Joseph alone can appreciate.
14: Joseph's test-declaration: "it is just as I said to you — you are spies." The false accusation is maintained; Joseph is creating a structure that will bring Benjamin to Egypt without revealing his identity or his plan. The narrative does not tell us whether Joseph had a complete plan at this point or improvised; what is clear is that his response is purposeful: the imprisonment, the three-day wait, and the Simeon-hostage are all elements of a test designed to bring about the family's full reunion.
15: חֵי פַרְעֹה (ḥê parʿōh, as Pharaoh lives) — the Egyptian royal oath formula; cf. Genesis 42:16; Genesis 44:5. Joseph swears by Pharaoh's life, not by God's — he is operating within the Egyptian official context. The oath by Pharaoh's life is the highest civil oath available in Egypt; it carries the full weight of royal authority and is binding. Contrasted with Genesis 42:18 where Joseph shifts to "I fear God" — the two registers (Egyptian oath and divine fear) coexist in Joseph's speech.
16: שִׁלְחוּ מִכֶּם אֶחָד וְיִקַּח אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם — "send one of you to get your brother." The test conditions: one brother goes to Canaan; the others are imprisoned while the truth of their claim is checked. Wĕʾattem hēʾāsĕrû (and you will be bound/imprisoned) — ʾāsar (to bind, imprison — the same root as the "binding" of Simeon in Genesis 42:24; the brothers who are imprisoned will know, from the inside, what Joseph experienced in his years of imprisonment).
17: וַיֶּאֱסֹף אֹתָם אֶל-מִשְׁמָר שְׁלֹשֶׁת יָמִים (wayyeʾĕsōp ʾōtām ʾel-mišmār šĕlōšet yāmîm) — "he put them all in custody for three days." Mišmār (custody, watch — a guarded confinement; cf. Genesis 40:3, 4, 7 where the same word describes the prison where Joseph himself was kept). The three days (šĕlōšet yāmîm) — a significant interval in the Joseph narrative (cf. Genesis 40:13, 19-20 where three days is the predictive timeline; and 22:4 where the three-day journey to Moriah). Three days of imprisonment gives the brothers time to experience what Joseph experienced.
18: אֶת-הָאֱלֹהִים אֲנִי יָרֵא (ʾet-hāʾĔlōhîm ʾănî yārēʾ) — "God I fear." The emphatic word order: ʾĔlōhîm is first in the clause (object before subject and verb) — God is syntactically first; ʾănî (I) is second; yārēʾ (fear, Qal participle) is third. The divine fear (yirʾat ʾĔlōhîm) is the foundation of Joseph's ethics throughout: in Genesis 39 he refused Potiphar's wife by appealing to God (Genesis 39:9: "how could I do this wicked thing and sin against God?"); now he identifies the same fear as the motive for modifying the harsh test-terms. The yirʾat ʾĔlōhîm is the governing moral principle of the Joseph story.
19: אַחֵיכֶם הָאֶחָד יֵאָסֵר (ʾaḥêḵem hāʾeḥāḏ yēʾāsēr) — "one of your brothers will be bound." Joseph modifies the plan: instead of one going free while nine are imprisoned, nine will go free while one stays. The modification is presented as an act of ʾĔlōhîm-fearing mercy: keeping nine imprisoned would risk the starvation of their households. Lĕʿammêḵem (for your starving households) — rāʿāḇ (the famine word applied to their families: "your starving households").
20: וְאֶת-אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן תָּבִיאוּ אֵלַי (wĕʾet-ʾăḥîḵem haqqāṭōn tāḇîʾû ʾēlay) — "bring your youngest brother to me." Qāṭōn (small, youngest — cf. the earlier use: David is the "youngest" [haqqāṭōn] brother [1 Samuel 17:14]; the youngest is often the one who reverses expectations). Benjamin is the key: bringing Benjamin proves the brothers' story and sets up the second phase of Joseph's test. Wĕyēʾāmĕnû diḇrêḵem wĕlōʾ tāmûtû (your words will be verified and you will not die) — life and death hang on the word-verification; the test has real stakes.
21: אֲבָל אֲשֵׁמִים אֲנַחְנוּ (ʾăḇāl ʾăšēmîm ʾănaḥnû) — "surely we are guilty." ʾĂḇāl (truly, surely — an asseverative particle of strong affirmation, often introducing a counter-truth: "but in fact..." [cf. Genesis 17:19; Genesis 28:16; 37:33 where ʾăḵēn serves a similar function]). ʾĀšēm (guilty — Qal active participle of ʾāšam, to be guilty, to incur guilt; the root of the guilt-offering [ʾāšām] of Leviticus; this is the only occurrence of this vocabulary in Genesis: the brothers use the technical guilt-language to describe their moral condition). אֲשֶׁר רָאִינוּ צָרַת נַפְשׁוֹ בְּהִתְחַנְּנוֹ אֵלֵינוּ (we saw his soul's distress when he pleaded with us) — the first retrospective confirmation of Genesis 37's pit scene: the brothers saw Joseph's ṣārat nepeš (distress of soul — ṣārāh, distress + nepeš, soul/self; the innermost suffering); and they heard bĕhiṯḥannĕnô (when he pleaded — Hitpael of ḥānan, to show grace, to beg: the intense reflexive-intensive of the grace-petition; Joseph literally begged for mercy). The narrator did not report this in Genesis 37; now the brothers' guilty memory reveals it twenty-two years later.
22: הֲלוֹא אָמַרְתִּי אֲלֵיכֶם לֵאמֹר אַל-תֶּחֶטְאוּ בַיָּלֶד (hălōʾ ʾāmartî ʾălêḵem lēʾmōr ʾal-teḥĕṭĕʾû ḇayyāleḏ) — "did I not say to you 'do not sin against the boy'?" ḥāṭāʾ (to sin — the standard sin-verb; Reuben frames the sale of Joseph as sin against the boy; the guilt-vocabulary intensifies: ʾāšēmîm [Genesis 42:21] + teḥĕṭĕʾû [Genesis 42:22]). דָּמוֹ הִנֵּה נִדְרָשׁ (dāmô hinnēh niḏrāš) — "now his blood is being required." Nāḏaš (Niphal of dāraš, to seek, require — passive: his blood is demanded; the accountability for bloodshed; cf. Genesis 9:5: "your blood I will require" [ʾeḏrōš] — the Noahic covenant's bloodguilt principle is invoked by Reuben; the same root).
23: כִּי הַמֵּלִיץ בֵּינֹתָם (kî hammēlîṣ bênōtām) — "because an interpreter was between them." Mēlîṣ (interpreter/go-between — from a root that can mean to scoff/mock [cf. Proverbs 3:34; Psalms 1:1] but in the Hiphil can mean to interpret/translate; in this passage, "interpreter" is the standard and contextually obvious reading). Joseph uses an interpreter to maintain his Egyptian identity; his brothers speak freely in Hebrew, assuming they are not understood. The dramatic irony: the brothers' guilt-speech, their genuine repentance-language, is overheard by the very man they wronged — who understood every word.
24: וַיִּסֹּב מֵעֲלֵיהֶם וַיֵּבְךְּ (wayyissōḇ mēʿălêhem wayyēḇkĕ) — "he turned away from them and wept." Sāḇaḇ (to turn, go around — he turned away from their sight); bāḵāh (to weep — Joseph's first of five weeping scenes in the Joseph narrative: Genesis 42:24; Genesis 43:30; Genesis 45:2; Genesis 45:14-15; Genesis 46:29). The weeping is private — he turns away first; the public face returns composed. This pattern (private weeping → controlled public return) characterizes Joseph throughout: the emotional reality is genuine and intense; the public management is strategic. וַיִּקַּח מֵאִתָּם אֶת-שִׁמְעוֹן (he took Simeon from them and bound him before their eyes) — Simeon, the second-born; Reuben (the firstborn) spoke for rescue in Genesis 37:21-22; Judah (the fourth) proposed the sale in Genesis 37:26-27; Simeon is neither the rescuer nor the ringleader but is selected as the hostage, possibly as the oldest brother available (Reuben must return to Jacob) or as the one associated with violent revenge (Genesis 34:25).
25: לְמַלֵּא אֶת-כְּלֵיהֶם בָּר וּלְהָשִׁיב כַּסְפֵּיהֶם (lĕmallēʾ ʾet-kĕlêhem bār ûlĕhāšîḇ kaspêhem) — "to fill their vessels with grain and to return their silver." The silver return is Joseph's covert act of providence — or test; the brothers do not know it is happening. Kāsep (silver — the purchase-price of grain; but also the silver for which Joseph was sold: twenty shekels of silver [Genesis 37:28]; the silver motif returns). Wĕlāṯet lāhem ṣēḏāh laddāreḵ (and to give them provisions for the journey) — additional provisions beyond the grain; Joseph is feeding them even as he tests them.
26: They load the grain on their donkeys and depart. The departure is businesslike; the emotional scene of Simeon's binding (Genesis 42:24) is followed immediately by the practical loading and leaving. The narrative speed — loading, departing — mirrors the brothers' desire to leave an uncomfortable situation. The silver in the sack is unknown to them at this point.
27: וַיַּרְא אֶת-כַּסְפּוֹ וְהִנֵּה-הוּא בְּפִי אַמְתַּחְתּוֹ (wayyarʾ ʾet-kaspô wĕhinnēh-hûʾ bĕpî ʾamtaḥtô) — "he saw his silver — and there it was in the mouth of his sack." The wĕhinnēh (and behold!) of sudden, startling discovery; the silver appears unexpectedly at the stopping-place for the night. Pî ʾamtaḥtô (the mouth of his sack — peh = mouth; ʾamtaḥat = sack, bag [appearing only in the Joseph narrative]; the mouth/opening of the bag). The silver in the mouth of the sack is a narrative set-piece that creates maximum anxiety: what should be at the bottom (grain) is at the top (silver).
28: מַה-זֹּאת עָשָׂה אֱלֹהִים לָנוּ (mah-zōʾt ʿāśāh ʾĔlōhîm lānû) — "what is this that God has done to us?" The brothers immediately interpret the silver as divine action. Their guilt-ridden consciousness reads every unexplained event as judgment: they cannot conceive of an innocent explanation. The question echoes the brothers' earlier question-of-judgment (they said to each other in Genesis 42:21 "we are guilty"); now they interpret the silver as God's intervention in their case. The irony: it is God's action through Joseph, but not judgment — it is providential care that Joseph provides, even while testing them. Wayyēṣēʾ libbām (their hearts went out/sank — literally "went out"; lēḇ [heart] going out = an idiom for sudden terror; cf. Joshua 2:11 where the Canaanites' "heart went out" when they heard of Israel's crossing the Red Sea).
29: The brothers return to Jacob in Canaan and give a full account of "all that had happened to them." The narrative phrase ʾēt kol-haqqōrōt ʾōtām (all that had befallen them — qārāh, to happen/befall; the events of their Egypt journey are summarized before the detailed retelling of Genesis 42:30-34). The two-level narration (summary + detail) is the pattern of the servant's retelling in Genesis 24 — embedded retelling that allows the reader to hear the events interpreted through the brothers' own frame.
30: דִּבֶּר אִתָּנוּ קָשׁוֹת (dĭbber ʾittānû qāšôt) — "he spoke harshly/hard things with us." The brothers' retelling characterizes Joseph's speech as qāšôt (hard, harsh words — the same adjective as in Genesis 42:7; the brothers accurately report the harshness of Joseph's treatment). Hāʾîš ʾăḏōnê hāʾāreṣ (the man who is lord of the land) — they describe Joseph by his title and function, not his identity; to them he is "the lord of the land" — the Egyptian official; they have no idea he is Joseph.
31: The brothers' self-defense in their report mirrors their defense to Joseph (Genesis 42:10-11): "we said we are honest men, not spies." Kĕnîm ʾănaḥnû (we are honest men) — the identical formula; their defense was consistent; they are reporting accurately what they said. The retelling confirms their integrity in the encounter even while the narrative reader knows the deep irony: the accused spies are the actual wrongdoers against Joseph, though not in the way Joseph charged them.
32: אֶחָד אֵינֶנּוּ (ʾeḥāḏ ʾênennû, one is no more) — the brothers report their own words to Joseph about the missing brother; "one is no more" — the same formula of Genesis 37:30. They tell their father that they reported Joseph as dead to the Egyptian lord. The double layer of the original lie (to Jacob) now becomes a public fact, retold in Jacob's presence, without knowing Joseph is alive. The concealment of the sale has become part of their family narrative: the missing brother is simply "no more."
33: The brothers retell Joseph's test-conditions: leave one brother as pledge; take grain to your starving households; bring the youngest brother. The retelling to Jacob recapitulates the test for the reader and prepares the conflict of Genesis 42:36-38: Jacob must decide whether to release Benjamin.
34: אֶת-אֲחִיכֶם הַקָּטֹן (your youngest brother) — the demand for Benjamin is the hinge of the entire narrative: without Benjamin, the family cannot return to Egypt; without return to Egypt, they cannot eat; without eating, they will die; but sending Benjamin risks the life that Jacob has protected since Rachel's death. The test-structure forces Jacob to choose between Benjamin and the family's survival.
35: הִנֵּה אִישׁ צְרוֹר-כַּסְפּוֹ בְּשַׂקּוֹ (hinnēh ʾîš ṣĕrôr-kaspô bĕśakkô) — "there in each man's sack was his pouch of silver!" Ṣĕrôr (bundle, tied-up pouch — a small bundle of money; cf. Song of Solomon 1:13 where the myrrh is a ṣĕrôr; Proverbs 7:20 where money is carried in a ṣĕrôr). Wayyirʾû ʾet-ṣĕrōrôt kaspêhem hēmmāh wĕʾăḇîhem wayyîrāʾû (when they and their father saw the money pouches, they were afraid) — the fear is shared between brothers and father; the silver is inexplicable and ominous; Jacob is seeing what the one brother saw at the lodging (Genesis 42:27-28), now multiplied tenfold.
36: אֹתִי שִׁכַּלְתֶּם (ʾōtî šikkaltĕm) — "you have bereaved me." Šāḵal (Piel: to bereave — the verb used for the loss of children; cf. Lamentations 1:20; Jeremiah 15:7; here the Piel is intense and accusatory: "you have made me childless"). Jacob's accusation is not entirely accurate or fair — the brothers did not cause Joseph's disappearance knowingly in Jacob's presence, though they did sell him. כֻּלָּנָה הָיוּ עָלָי (kullānāh hāyû ʿālay) — "everything is against me!" — kullānāh (all these things — the feminine plural of kōl); the suffix -nāh is an intensive/archaic form. Jacob's declaration of total loss is the low point of his perspective: subjectively everything is gone. The narrative irony: everything is in fact working toward reunion.
37: אֶת-שְׁנֵי בָנַי תָּמִית (ʾet-šĕnê ḇānay tāmît) — "you may put both of my sons to death." Reuben's desperate surety-offer: his two sons as pledge for Benjamin's safety. Tāmît (Hiphil of mût, to put to death — the causative; Reuben gives Jacob the right to execute his own grandchildren if he fails). The offer is excessive and reveals Reuben's desperation; it is also strategically ineffective — killing Reuben's sons would add to Jacob's bereavement, not compensate it. Jacob does not even engage with the specific terms of Reuben's offer.
38: לֹא-יֵרֵד בְּנִי עִמָּכֶם (lōʾ-yēreḏ bĕnî ʿimmāḵem) — "my son will not go down with you." The flat refusal; Jacob uses the singular bĕnî (my son) — in his grief, only Benjamin is "my son"; the others are being addressed as "you." כִּי-אָחִיו מֵת וְהוּא לְבַדּוֹ נִשְׁאָר (for his brother is dead and he alone remains) — the statement "his brother is dead" is Jacob's belief about Joseph; it is also the brothers' lie maintained for twenty-two years. תּוֹרִידוּ אֶת-שֵׂיבָתִי בְּיָגוֹן שְׁאֹלָה (you will bring my gray head down to Sheol in sorrow) — the same sentence as Genesis 37:35 (ʾēreḏ ʾel-bĕnî ʾāḇēl šĕʾōlāh); the formula of grief-unto-death is Jacob's refrain across the Joseph story; śêḇāh (gray hair — the white hair of old age; the vulnerable aged parent). The chapter ends in an impasse: Egypt requires Benjamin; Jacob refuses to send him; Simeon remains in prison; the family will starve unless the impasse breaks.
